---
title: "Introduction to R"
subtitle: "For the R-Curious"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, styles.scss]
    self-contained: true
    chalkboard: false
    standalone: true
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: png
    dev.args: { bg: "transparent" }
---

```{r}
#| messages: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
```


## Who are we?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Dr. Elizabeth King** (she/her)

- Grew up in Southeastern Wisconsin (until 7th grade) $\rightarrow$ Chicago-land
- Iowa (4 years) $\rightarrow$ California (10 years) $\rightarrow$ Missouri
- MU 12 years in Biology
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Dr. Kevin Middleton** (he/him)

- Grew up in Florida
- North Carolina $\rightarrow$ Rhode Island (10 years) $\rightarrow$ California (1 year) $\rightarrow$ Rhode Island (1 year) $\rightarrow$ California (5 years) $\rightarrow$ Missouri
- MU 14 years (10 in Medicine, 4 in Biology)

:::

::::

<hr />

. . .

- Biologists by training
- Engineers, theoreticians, statisticians, and programmers by avocation
- We are married.


## Why are we here?

- Programming/data skills are increasingly important for you as researchers, regardless of field
- Making research more reproducible benefits all of us
- We have a strong interest in empowering trainees to gain data science skills
- You might find that you really enjoy it


## Kevin's History

- Kid:
    - Computers around
    - Lots of programming
- Graduate student:
    - Refused to take statisticsDid a little "analysis automation" with scripts
    - Tried R a few times and gave up -- "What am I supposed to do with this: `>`?"


## Kevin's History

- Post-doc:
    - SAS / SPSS,
    - Tried R a few more times and gave up
        - Needed to do something that was only in R
        - Learned enough R to do that one thing
        - Liked it and learned more
- Faculty member:
    - Didn't want to pay for SAS / SPSS
    - Didn't want to tie students to non-free software


## First script {.smaller}

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

#file /Users/kmm/Documents/Work/Projects/Bone/g16_bone - 20 mo (Middleton)/R/qvalue analysis.r

# Created: 31 Jan 2006 by Kevin Middleton
# Modifications:
#		19 Feb 2006: Added expected null line to histogram.
#		20 Feb 2006: Added "prob=TRUE" and "las=1" arguments to
#			hist plotting function. Histogram is now relative (1=null).
#			pi0 now easy to plot on histogram with abline(h=pi0)
#		21 Feb 2006: Added analyses 2 (bootstrapping) & 3 (lambda = 0).
#		22 Mar 2006: Added comparative analyses at end (robust, various FDR)
#		10 Aug 2006: Now loading pvalues_final.txt

# False discovery rate on g16 bone p values
# Data are derived from final tables (1 & 3) of g16 bone ms.

library(qvalue)

setwd(
  "/Users/kmm/Documents/Work/Projects/Bone/g16_bone - 20 mo (Middleton)/R"
)

# Read the data in to a vector
# bonep_reduced_further.txt is a plain text file with 1 p value per row
# 	This file was created using the p vaules from the final tables of the
#	g16 bone manuscript
pvals <- scan("pvalues_final.txt")

pvals <- sort(pvals, decreasing = TRUE)

# Check distribution of p values.
#pdf(file="/Users/kmm/Documents/Work/R/FDR/hedenfalk/g16_histogram2.pdf", height=6, width=6, horizontal=F)
hist(
  pvals,
  breaks = 20,
  prob = TRUE,
  las = 1,
  xlab = "p value",
  ylab = "Relative Frequency",
  main = ""
)
abline(h = 1, col = 4, lty = 2, lwd = 2)
abline(h = .704, col = "red", lty = 3, lwd = 2)
#dev.off()
#abline(h=length(pvals)/20,col=4,lty=2)

######## ANALYSIS 1 - DEFAULT PARAMETERS ########
# Perform FDR using default parameters:
#	lambda = seq(0, 0.9, 0.05)
#	pi0.method = "smoother"
#	fdr.level = NULL
#	robust = FALSE
#	smooth.df = 3
#	smooth.log.pi0 = FALSE
qobj.def <- qvalue(pvals)

# Open a new plotting window
quartz(width = 7, height = 7)

# Plot the qvalue diagnostic graphs
qplot(qobj.def)

# Print out qvalue summary information
qsummary(qobj.def)

# Need to learn what this value represents
1 - qobj.def$pi0

# Calculate new alpha level
max(qobj.def$pvalues[qobj.def$qvalues <= 0.05])

# Write output to text file
qwrite(qobj.def, filename = "qvalue_default_settings.txt")


######## ANALYSIS 2 - pi0.method = "bootstrap" ########
# Dabney & Storey (qvalue manual) say that for small numbers of p values
#	the "bootstrap" methods described in Storey et al. (2004) is a better
#	estimator of pi0

# Perform FDR with bootstrapping:
#	pi0.method = "bootstrap"
qobj.boot <- qvalue(pvals, pi0.method = "bootstrap")

# Open a new plotting window
quartz(width = 7, height = 7)

# Plot the qvalue diagnostic graphs
#pdf(file="/Users/kmm/Documents/Work/R/FDR/hedenfalk/g16_qplot.pdf", height=6, width=6, horizontal=F)
qplot(qobj.boot)
#dev.off()

# Print out qvalue summary information
qsummary(qobj.boot)

# Need to learn what this value represents
1 - qobj.boot$pi0

# Calculate new alpha level
max(qobj.boot$pvalues[qobj.boot$qvalues <= 0.05])

# Write output to text file
qwrite(qobj.boot, filename = "qvalue_bootstrap.txt")


######## ANALYSIS 3 - Benjamini & Hochberg (1995) ########
# Dabney & Storey (qvalue manual) state that setting lamdba = 0
#	will force pi0 = 1, approximating the assumptions implicit in
# 	Benjamini & Hochberg (1995)

# Perform FDR with lambda = 0:
#	lambda = 0
qobj.bh <- qvalue(pvals, lambda = 0)

# Open a new plotting window
quartz(width = 7, height = 7)

# Plot the qvalue diagnostic graphs
qplot(qobj.bh)

# Print out qvalue summary information
qsummary(qobj.bh)

# Need to learn what this value represents
1 - qobj.bh$pi0

# Calculate new alpha level
max(qobj.bh$pvalues[qobj.bh$qvalues <= 0.05])

# Write output to text file
qwrite(qobj.bh, filename = "qvalue_bh.txt")


qobj.boot <- qvalue(pvals, pi0.method = "bootstrap", fdr.level = 0.05)
qobj.boot.robust <- qvalue(
  pvals,
  pi0.method = "bootstrap",
  robust = TRUE,
  fdr.level = 0.05
)
qobj.lambda0_0.05 <- qvalue(pvals, lambda = 0, fdr.level = 0.05)
qobj.lambda0_0.10 <- qvalue(pvals, lambda = 0, fdr.level = 0.10)
qwrite(qobj.boot, file = "qobj.boot.txt")
qwrite(qobj.boot.robust, file = "qobj.boot.robust.txt")
qwrite(qobj.lambda0_0.05, file = "qobj.lambda0_0.05.txt")
qwrite(qobj.lambda0_0.10, file = "qobj.lambda0_0.10.txt")
```


## When did I feel comfortable teaching with R?

- 2010: First graduate course
- 2011-2012: Undergraduate courses
- 2016: First graduate course at MU
- 2017: Start co-teaching Quantitative Methods in Life Sciences
- 2025-2026: Undergraduate courses at MU


## Why are you here?


## Outline

- Example use cases
- Introduction to R/RStudio & Reproducibility
- Roadmaps for different levels of learners
- Roadmaps for different specific skills


# Use Cases


## Creating a data sheet (EGK)

```{r}
#| echo: true

reps <- paste0("R", 1:3)
treats <- c("C", "DR", "HS")
lines <- c(
  paste(1:12, "DA", sep = "."),
  paste(1:12, "FA", sep = "."),
  paste(1:12, "HS", sep = ".")
)
dat.tab <- crossing(lines, treats, reps) |>
  arrange(reps)
box.r <- tibble(
  box = rep(paste0("box_", 1:4), each = 100),
  x = rep(rep(1:10, each = 10), 4),
  y = rep(rep(1:10, 10), 4)
)
box.r <- box.r[
  c(sample(1:100), sample(101:200), sample(201:300), sample(301:400)),
]
dat.tab <- cbind(dat.tab, box.r[1:nrow(dat.tab), ])
write_csv(dat.tab, "UseCase_datatable1.csv")

dat.tab |>
  arrange(box, x, y) |>
  write_csv("UseCase_datatable2.csv")

```

## Joining & pivoting (EGK)

- note - one ds for set up, one for data in cols
- join on set up date
- pivot on cols

## Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r}
ds <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/QM-Life-Sci/RDABR/refs/heads/main/data/Datasaurus_data.csv",
  col_names = c("x", "y"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(head(ds))
```

Correlation test:

```{r}
cor.test(ds$x, ds$y)
```


## Exploratory Data Analysis

```{r}
#| fig-alt: "Scatterplot that looks like a dinosaur."

ggplot(ds, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = "navy") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(
    caption = expression(paste("Alberto Cairo's ", italic("Datasaurus")))
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```


## Finding Errors (KMM)

## Doing the same thing to lots of things (EGK)


## Making beautiful figures (KMM)

![](https://cdn.fosstodon.org/media_attachments/files/116/209/557/460/653/062/original/ce83839bc0622920.png)


## Stats (the hard way now is the easy way later) & redoing stats & analysis reports (EGK)


## Reproducible instructions & code (KMM)


## Slides & presentations & papers & websites & CV (KMM)


## Introduction to R/RStudio & Reproducibility

- R is a general purpose programming language (that is optimized for statistics and data visualization)
- You can do all of these things in other languages (some easier and many more difficult)
- R enforces an "ask for what you want" statistical approach


# Roadmaps for different levels of learners

## What is your current skill level/experience?

## Roadmaps for different levels of learners
QR code for each then slide with grid of instructions

# Roadmaps for different specific skills

## What do you most want to learn/get better at?




## Slide 2

[Quarto]{.blue} enables you to weave together [content]{.yellow} and [executable code]{.yellow} into a **finished presentation**. To learn more about [Quarto]{.blue} presentations see <https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/>.

## Fenced divs

::: blue
Some paragraph.
:::

or

::: {.yellow}
Some paragraph.
:::

## Links

https://slidecrafting-book.com/scss
